ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for South Indian dry pepper fish preparation; closest_parent 'fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for dry pepper-spiced fish in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 'Fish dish (food)' is the closest parent; no specific concept for South Indian dry pepper fish exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Pepper Fish (Dry) is approximately 200 g, consisting of several bite-sized fish cubes coated in a dry black pepper and spice crust.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, rohu, cooked / Spiced fish preparation — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), adapted for dry pepper fish
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Pepper Fish (Dry)' exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked fish entries (rohu/catla, cooked) and USDA FoodData Central 'Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat' (FDC ID 175168), adjusted upward for oil absorption (~5 g fat/100 g added) and sodium from spice marinade. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is an estimated composition; values should be verified against laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fish and protein-dominant dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for South Indian dry pepper fish. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Fish and protein-dominant preparations with minimal starchy carbohydrate consistently yield GI values in the 20–40 range. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (30 × 3.5 × 2) / 100 = 2.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and added salt (~420 mg/100 g; ~840 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less per meal, as a full 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced salt in preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g/100 g) with high protein (~19.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pepper Fish (Dry) is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI of approximately 30 and minimal carbohydrate content, resulting in a glycemic load of only ~2.1 per serving; the high protein content also supports satiety and glycemic stability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (~10.5 g/100 g) with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish, offset by cooking oil addition and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish base provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein; however, the added cooking oil increases total fat to ~10.5 g/100 g and sodium reaches ~420 mg/100 g, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume moderate portions (100–150 g) and prefer preparations using minimal oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pepper Fish (Dry) is a South Indian spiced fish dish consisting of bite-sized cubes of firm-fleshed fish — typically white fish such as rohu, catla, or kingfish — marinated in a bold dry spice blend dominated by coarsely ground black pepper, turmeric, red chilli, curry leaves, ginger, and garlic, then pan-tossed or stir-fried in oil until the spices form a dry, aromatic crust on the fish surface. The preparation is characteristic of South Indian coastal cuisine, particularly Tamil Nadu and Kerala styles, and is served as a side dish or starter alongside rice, biryani, or flatbreads. The texture is firm and slightly chewy with a crisp spiced exterior; the flavour profile is intensely peppery, mildly hot, and savoury with earthy spice notes from turmeric and curry leaves. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~19.5 g/100 g), moderate in fat (~10.5 g/100 g) primarily from cooking oil, and very low in carbohydrates (~3.5 g/100 g), yielding a low glycemic index of approximately 30. The sodium content is moderate to high (~420 mg/100 g) due to salt and spice marinade. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Indian restaurants and tiffin centres as part of a rice set or as a standalone protein dish. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium intake should be monitored by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Is pepper fish dry good for diabetics?
Pepper fish dry GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Pepper Fish (Dry)' exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting ICMR-NIN cooked fish data and USDA FoodData Central cooked fish values, with adjustments for oil absorption and spice-derived sodium. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant fish dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the base food (cooked fish) is well-characterised in approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (2017) cooked fish entries and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175168 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat), adapted for oil absorption (~5 g fat/100 g added from pan-frying) and sodium from spice marinade. Laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation is recommended for higher confidence. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted ICMR-NIN and USDA source entries used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entries. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entries. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as fish and vegetable cooking oils used in this preparation do not contribute measurable trans fats. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The dish is served at Bombay Grill (Restaurant Delivery) on field survey data at SGD 18.90. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the fish-only protein source and typical South Indian restaurant practice; kosher status is not confirmed.
